text: Robotic Suture Rectopexy for External Full Thickness Rectal Prolapse, a video vignette. Patient is a forty-eight-year-old female with past surgical history of hysterectomy and bladder sling who presented with fetal incontinence.

Robotic Suture Rectopexy for External Full Thickness Rectal Prolapse, a video vignette. Patient is a forty-eight-year-old female with past surgical history of hysterectomy and bladder sling who presented with fetal incontinence.

Preoperative workup included endoscopic anal ultrasound, anal manometry, and MRI defecography. Endoscopic anal ultrasound found an intact internal and external anal sphincter, with thinning of each. Anal manometry also reported decreased resting pressure but normal squeeze pressure and normal sensitivity.

Preoperative workup included endoscopic anal ultrasound, anal manometry, and MRI defecography. Endoscopic anal ultrasound found an intact internal and external anal sphincter, with thinning of each. Anal manometry also reported decreased resting pressure but normal squeeze pressure and normal sensitivity.

MRI defecography found severe to moderate tricompartmental descent with the additional findings consisting of a small cystocele, moderate anterior rectocele, and a rectal prolapse with the anal rectal junction descending six point one centimeters below the pubococcygeal line. The patient was evaluated in conjunction with urogynecology, and pelvic floor physical therapy was found to be indicated.

MRI defecography found severe to moderate tricompartmental descent with the additional findings consisting of a small cystocele, moderate anterior rectocele, and a rectal prolapse with the anal rectal junction descending six point one centimeters below the pubococcygeal line. The patient was evaluated in conjunction with urogynecology, and pelvic floor physical therapy was found to be indicated.
However, due to lack of improvement, it was decided to perform a combined robotic sacral copal pexy and robotic suture rectal pexy. This procedure was performed using a DaVinci XI robot.
However, due to lack of improvement, it was decided to perform a combined robotic sacral copal pexy and robotic suture rectal pexy. This procedure was performed using a DaVinci XI robot.

Four 8mm trocars are placed, and the surgeon incises the peritoneum on the right side of the rectum, positioned medial to the right ureter and lateral to the right hypogastric nerve, creating a peritoneal flap for suspension.

The surgeon places four 8mm trocars and incises the peritoneum on the right side of the rectum, medial to the right ureter and lateral to the right hypogastric nerve, creating a peritoneal flap for suspension.

The surgeon mobilizes the rectum circumferentially, beginning posteriorly through the holy plane and advancing towards the pouch of Douglas. The peritoneum between the vagina and rectum is incised, deepening the dissection until the levator ani muscles are identified, marking the extent of pelvic mobilization.

The surgeon mobilizes the rectum in a circumferential manner, beginning posteriorly through the holy plane and advancing towards the pouch of Douglas. The peritoneum between the vagina and rectum is incised, deepening the dissection until the levator ani muscles are identified, marking the extent of pelvic mobilization.

The surgeon verifies hemostasis and dissects the sacral promontory, freeing it from surrounding structures including the lumbosacral trunk laterally and hypogastric nerves medially. The peritoneal flaps of the rectum are then fixated to the sacral promontory using non-absorbable braided interrupted tuo sutures bilaterally.

The peritoneal flaps of the rectum are then fixated to the sacral promontory using non-absorbable braided interrupted tuo sutures bilaterally.
The surgeon performs a dissection to the left of the rectum, mirroring the previous dissection on the right side, creating a peritoneal flap for suspension.
The surgeon performs a dissection to the left of the rectum, mirroring the previous dissection on the right side, to create a peritoneal flap for suspension.
The surgeon closes the opening in the peritoneum using non-absorbable running sutures, with the area indicated by an arrow for precise suture placement.
The surgeon closes the opening in the peritoneum using non-absorbable running sutures, as indicated by an arrow.

Postoperatively, the patient recovered without complications and was discharged home. On postoperative visits, the patient expressed satisfaction with cosmetic result without recurrence or constipation.
